mechanism_summary: Gut dysbiosis → reduced SCFA production → increased intestinal permeability → LPS translocation → TLR4 activation → NF-κB → TNF-α, IL-6 → sebocyte inflammation and hyperproliferation
Gut dysbiosis correlates with SIBO (46-66% of rosacea patients); dysbiosis drives systemic inflammation and neurovascular reactivity
Early gut dysbiosis precedes AD development; reduced microbial diversity in infancy correlates with AD risk; SCFA deficiency impairs Treg function and barrier genes
Psoriasis patients show reduced F. prausnitzii and increased Proteobacteria; bacterial translocation → Th17/IL-23 pathway activation
Gut dysbiosis may influence immune regulation; Malassezia overgrowth on skin may correlate with gut microbiome shifts
SIBO in 46-66% of rosacea patients vs 5-27% controls; bacterial overgrowth → inflammation → neurovascular activation; rifaximin treatment clears rosacea in 70-100%
Elevated serum LPS and LBP in acne patients; LPS translocation → TLR4 activation on keratinocytes and sebocytes → IL-8, TNF-α, MMP-9
Increased intestinal permeability (lactulose:mannitol ratio) correlates with psoriasis severity; bacterial translocation → Th17 activation
Intestinal permeability allows allergen penetration → systemic immune activation → Th2 skewing and IgE elevation → skin inflammation
Hyperinsulinemia → elevated IGF-1 → mTORC1 activation → SREBP-1 → lipogenesis/sebum overproduction + keratinocyte hyperproliferation; also reduces SHBG → more free androgens
citation: hormone-skin-connection.md: Section 1.5; skin-condition-profiles.md: Acne root causes

mechanism_summary: Insulin and IGF-1 directly stimulate keratinocyte and fibroblast proliferation in flexural areas → velvety hyperpigmented plaques
Insulin reduces SHBG → more free testosterone → androgen receptor activation in hair follicles
Insulin and IGF-1 stimulate fibroblast and keratinocyte proliferation in skin folds
Hyperglycemia → non-enzymatic glycation → AGE formation on collagen → cross-linking → stiffness, reduced elasticity, yellowing
Cortisol stimulates sebaceous glands; increases androgen production; chronic stress elevates cortisol 50-100% above baseline
Cortisol → barrier compromise → allergen penetration → inflammation; stress precedes 67% of eczema flares
Stress → sympathetic activation → IL-6, TNF-α release → keratinocyte hyperproliferation; 40-80% of patients report stress as trigger
Stress may trigger autoimmune attack on hair follicles via immune dysregulation; many patients report significant stress preceding onset
Stress is #1 rosacea trigger; CRH stimulates mast cells → flushing, inflammation, vascular reactivity
Cortisol binds GR in fibroblasts → upregulates MMP-1, MMP-3, MMP-9 → collagen degradation; suppresses procollagen gene transcription → 30-40% reduced collagen synthesis
Exogenous or endogenous cortisol excess (Cushing's) → dermal collagen and elastin degradation → striae rubrae formation
Estrogen drops 90%+ post-menopause → collagen loss 2-5% per year (first 5 years), skin thinning 1-2% per year, HA depletion, reduced elasticity
Estrogen supports HA production, ceramide synthesis, and tight junction proteins; deficiency → dryness, reduced barrier function
Loss of estrogen-driven collagen synthesis (types I and III) and MMP-1 suppression → net collagen loss → dermal thinning
Estrogen stimulates melanogenesis via MITF upregulation; progesterone may synergize; hormonal contraceptives commonly trigger/exacerbate melasma
Relative estrogen excess or progesterone deficiency can contribute to cyclical skin changes; more commonly the imbalance ratio drives symptoms
DHT binds AR in sebocytes → lipogenic enzyme upregulation (FAS, ACC, SCD) → sebum overproduction; also upregulates TLR2 → enhanced inflammatory response to C. acnes
Androgens are primary driver of adult female jawline/chin acne; PCOS is major underlying cause (70-80% have acne)
citation: hormone-skin-connection.md: Section 1.4; clinical-guidelines.md: Acne hormonal therapy

mechanism_summary: DHT binds AR in hair follicle dermal papilla → conversion of vellus to terminal hairs in androgen-sensitive areas
DHT binds AR in scalp dermal papilla → shortens anagen phase → follicle miniaturization; increases TGF-β1/β2 → catagen promotion
Androgen-driven sebum provides substrate for Malassezia overgrowth
Hypothyroidism reduces sebum production, impairs barrier lipid synthesis, and reduces epidermal turnover → severe dryness and scaling
Both hypo- and hyperthyroidism cause diffuse non-scarring alopecia; hypothyroidism also causes brittle hair and nails
Chronic low-grade inflammation maintains Th17/IL-23 axis; elevated TNF-α, IL-6 perpetuate keratinocyte hyperproliferation
Systemic inflammation exacerbates Th2 response; elevated IL-6 and TNF-α worsen barrier dysfunction and immune dysregulation
Systemic inflammation amplifies local inflammatory response to C. acnes; elevated IL-1β and TNF-α drive lesion formation
Chronic low-grade inflammation ('inflammaging') drives MMP activation, collagen degradation, and cellular senescence
Elevated inflammatory cytokines impair normal wound healing phases; chronic inflammation shifts macrophage phenotype to M1 (pro-inflammatory) rather than M2 (pro-repair)
IA1 phylotype dominance (60-90% of acne lesion isolates) → hyaluronate lyase, CAMP factors, porphyrins → TLR2/TLR4 → IL-8, TNF-α
S. aureus colonization increases from 5% to >90% during AD flares; α-hemolysin damages keratinocytes; enterotoxins act as superantigens
S. aureus (and Streptococcus pyogenes) are primary pathogens; exfoliative toxins A/B cleave desmoglein 1
M. globosa lipases break down sebum triglycerides → oleic acid release → stratum corneum disruption and irritation
M. globosa overgrowth produces azelaic acid → hypopigmentation; also affects melanocyte function
Malassezia proliferation within follicles triggers neutrophilic inflammation → monomorphic papules/pustules on trunk
Demodex density 5-10× higher in papulopustular rosacea; mite-associated Bacillus oleronius triggers TLR2-mediated inflammation
D. folliculorum contributes to chronic lid margin inflammation; demodex debris blocks meibomian glands
UV-generated ROS directly damage collagen, activate MMPs, and cause DNA mutations; cumulative effect over years
ROS activate melanocytes and upregulate MITF; oxidative stress in melanocytes triggers compensatory melanin production
Inflammation-generated ROS trigger melanocyte activation and melanin deposition at sites of injury
ROS cause DNA damage (CPDs, 8-oxo-guanine); oxidative damage to p53 and other tumor suppressors → malignant transformation
citation: lifestyle-skin.md: Section 3 (UV → DNA damage); clinical-guidelines.md: Skin cancer screening

mechanism_summary: AGEs cross-link collagen and elastin → stiffness, reduced elasticity, yellowing; accelerated 2-3× in diabetes and insulin resistance
Zinc inhibits 5α-reductase (weak), NF-κB, and has direct antibacterial effect against C. acnes; deficiency worsens inflammation
Zinc essential for barrier repair, immune function, and filaggrin expression; deficiency impairs wound healing
Zinc is cofactor for collagen synthesis, DNA polymerase, and immune cell function; deficiency severely delays healing
Vitamin D suppresses Th17 and promotes Treg via VDR; deficiency removes this regulatory brake on inflammation
VDR activation in keratinocytes promotes antimicrobial peptide production and barrier function; deficiency associated with increased AD severity
Omega-3 deficiency skews eicosanoid balance toward pro-inflammatory leukotriene B4; omega-3s produce anti-inflammatory resolvins
Omega-3s are structural components of barrier lipids and precursors for anti-inflammatory mediators; deficiency impairs barrier and increases inflammation
Omega-3 fatty acids are incorporated into ceramides and other barrier lipids; deficiency reduces barrier integrity and increases TEWL
